title: Part 16
text: hence the notion of Homo Ludens: ‘Play is older than culture, for culture, however inadequately defined, always presupposes human society, and animals have not waited for man to teach them their playing’, as Huizinga put it. The spirit of playful competition is, as a social impulse, older than culture itself and pervades all life like a veritable ferment. Ritual grew up in sacred play; poetry was born in play and nourished on play; music and dancing were pure play ... . We have to conclude, therefore, that civilization is, in its earliest phases, played. It does not come from play ... it arises in and as play, and never leaves it. (Huizinga 1949: 21) It was exactly this dimension that Gregory Bateson incorporated in his epistemology, expressed in his famous essay On a Theory of Play and Fantasy (2000[1955]), among others. Drawing on his empirical research on animal communication, Bateson concluded that play (and even the use of metaphor) lies at the origin of both animal and human evolution, grounding our communication and learning processes. In all its seriousness, play is of course also fun; but again, this ‘fun’ 6 Voegelin reviewed Homo Ludens in Journal of Politics in 1948; it remained an important reference for Voegelin throughout his career, not least in the Order and History series. Voegelin praises Homo Ludens as ‘one of rare great works that advances substantially our understanding of man in historical and political existence’. Liminality and the Modern 150 cannot be broken down into categories and analytically dissected. It simply is; the fun is part of that ‘flow’ experience which Victor Turner later described. Huizinga identified five characteristics that play must have: 1. Play is free – is in fact freedom. 2. Play is not ‘ordinary’ or ‘real’ life. 3. Play is distinct from ‘ordinary’ life both as to locality and duration. 4. Play creates order, is order. Play demands order, absolute and supreme. 5. Play is connected with no material interest, and no profit can be gained from it. This is a meaningful characterization, both because it coheres with Plato’s analysis, and because it serves to stress the liminal nature of the playful as distinct from the ordinary in spatial, temporal and experiential terms. Play is a stepping- out of ‘real’ life into a temporary sphere of activity with a disposition all of its own. Importantly, Huizinga is expressly not saying that ‘everything is play’; the entire phenomenon is only possible because of distinction and the upholding of a boundary. As Huizinga puts it: ‘real civilization cannot exist in the absence of a certain play-element, for civilization presupposes limitation and mastery of the self, the ability not to confuse its own tendencies with the ultimate and highest goal, but to understand that it is enclosed within certain bounds freely accepted’ (1949: 211). The five points also read like this: no freedom, no play; no distinction between ordinary and extra-ordinary, no play; no spaces or times for play in distinction to normality, no play; no order, no play; if there is material interest or profit-making attached to the activity, then it is not play. Already here, of course, the problems pile up with respect to contemporary culture. Huizinga operates several other distinctions concerning play, and one is particularly important for our purposes here, namely that between competitive games and games of pure chance: In games of pure chance the tension felt by the player is only feebly communicated to the onlooker. In themselves, gambling games are very curious subjects for cultural research, but for the development of culture as such we must call them unproductive. They are sterile, adding nothing to life or mind. (Huizinga 1949: 48) This ‘unproductive’ nature of games of pure chance 7 should not let us underestimate their significance. In fact, in the first appendix to Man, Play and Games, Caillois 7 We use the term ‘pure chance’ to distinguish games like lottery and roulette from various forms of betting on events (such as horse races or football matches) where a degree of participation and involvement with the game often accompanies the gambling; these are still games of chance – and the participant is ‘passive’ – but not ‘pure’ chance. Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 151 discusses precisely The Importance of Games of Chance, seen from a comparative cultural perspective. Caillois notes how it is just such games of chance that under certain circumstances, ‘assumes sufficient importance to partly determine the life- style of an entire society’ (Caillois 2001: 145). One basic aspect of Bateson’s general communication theory must be stressed here, namely the role of frames. Play is defined by a frame around an activity, which changes the meaning of acts and words within that frame. Animals and humans do that in many ways, and most often the framing process is extra-linguistic. Animals know when they are playing for fun: cats avoid using their claws, for example. As Bateson showed, animals are very efficient in communicating to each other that ‘this is play’, and so are children. Adults are probably a bit slower on that point. Children sometimes verbalize the frame before they enter the game, for example: ‘Let us say that you were the mother and I was the baby’. I have noted how children almost always signal their frame by switching from present to past tense in most languages, and often also by altering the pitch of their voice just slightly. At certain moments of the play, they will turn to what Bateson calls meta-communication, as they address (with normal pitch and in present tense) some of the rules of the play, before they ‘enter’ the frame again: once in it, they are in it. It is very boring to play with people who don’t understand this; and children are very aware when adults lose play-concentration. The frame is a message about the message, and children know this by instinct. It is universal and no doubt perennial. Still with reference to the role of frames, one of Bateson’s further contributions was also to analyse what sometimes went wrong in communication processes. In fact, his paper on play and fantasy was inserted in Part III of Steps to an Ecology of Mind, called Form and Pathology in Relationship. Bateson famously applied his approach to schizophrenic persons. Very often, argued Bateson, maladjusted individuals can be diagnosed as having lost the capability to identify meaningful frames. That is why they feel lost about information, which they cannot place and decode meaningfully. That is also why they often lose humour (jokes are essentially frame-plays). Bateson also extended this analysis of distorted communication to the level of larger social patterns and civilizational derailment. The observation has general relevance: once we lose meaningful frames to define the playful act, then it leads to an instant loss of the playful itself. Let us now apply these conceptual dimensions to a history of the present. Play and the De-civilizing Process: The Ludic Fall of the Roman Empire We live in a period with a craving for games and excitement. There are antecedents to our present malaise and here again a long term comparative framework can be instructive. Before approaching the role of game and gambling in the transition to modernity, let us briefly consider how one can interpret the ludic element of ancient Roman civilization. As Mumford (1961) argued, outlining this development serves Liminality and the Modern 152 to formulate some warning signs concerning cultural-political developments of relevance to the present. The notion of play is expressed in Latin with the word ludens, and from here Huizinga took his title. The word is almost certainly of Etruscan origin. During the history of Ancient Rome a development took place which is indeed startling. The ludi were public Roman religious festivals, started before 220 bc as annual communal games to give thanks to the gods. The ludi romani was a Roman version of the Greek Olympic Games. In general the Roman ludi were public games for the Roman population. The earliest ludi were chariot races, held to celebrate victories in war. Originally, all ludi seem to have been votive offerings (ludi votivi), staged as the fulfilment of a vow to a deity whose favour had been sought and evidenced. As the product of military victory, ludi were often connected to triumphs. The first recorded venatio (staged beast hunt) was presented in 186 bc by M. Fulvius Nobilior as part of his ludi votivi, for which he paid with booty displayed at his triumph. After the Roman Republic, and increasingly during the Roman Empire, the ludi included gladiatorial combats or beast hunts. Therefore ludi were connected to and held at the same time as Roman religious festivals and thus became part of a cult of the state. As religious ceremonies, ludi were organized at first by various colleges of priests; during the Republic, they were later presented by consuls. The close connections between games, play, ritual and politics should be noted – as almost always. After horse races theatrical plays soon became popular also: ludi scaenici, theatrical performances. The most popular of these plays were Roman type comedies, as in the works of Plautus – and here we return to the theme of the mask, for Plautus developed all his comedies around the notion of masks. The story is one of progressive growth in numbers of people attending and days of the year dedicated to play. By the late imperial era the annual plays had expanded to include the following list, which is far from exhaustive: • Ludi Megalenses, 4–10 April, established 204 bc in honour of the Magna Mater in conjunction with the Megalensia. • Ludi Ceriales, 12–19April, established 202 bc in conjunction with the Cerealia, April 12. • Ludi Florales, 28 April–3 May, established 173 bc in honour of Flora, in conjunction with the Floralia, May 1 and characterised by its ‘atmosphere of primitive license and pastoral orgy’. • Ludi Apollinares, July 6–13, first celebrated in 211 bc in honour of Apollo to secure his aid against Hannibal, and made annual in 208 bc by senatorial decree. • Ludi Romani, September 4–19 in 44 bc, September 12–15 in the fourth century ad, established according to some legends in the sixth century bc in honour of Jupiter and at first held occasionally, not annually. • Ludi Augustales, October 3–12, established ad 14 after the death of Augustus and based on the Augustalia. Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 153 • Ludi Plebeii, originally November 13, on the Ides of Jupiter, and expanded to run November 4–17; established 216 bc and held in the Circus, and continued in the fourth century ad. To establish a fuller picture of the number of games within a given year, one should add to the list a whole series of games that were not held annually, but that were instead celebrated bi-annually or in some other cycle, plus a growing number of single-occasion ludi, often proposed by emperors to satisfy the population on an ad hoc basis. This intensified during the first century bc. It must be noted that there seems to exist a relationship between the fall of the Republic, the transition to empire and the proliferation of games. The proliferation of games was both a component and symptom of the decline and fall of the Roman Empire. As discussed by Szakolczai (2013, p. 95 ff), this relationship also extends to the sudden spread of pantomime, again testifying to the mimetic forces at play. The consolidation of this genre took place during the second half of the first century bc, and was simultaneous with the key periods in the establishment of permanent dictatorship and empire. When Rome was still functioning like a city-state, a certain degree of internal balance and harmony still reigned within the city. Citizenship was exercised with pride and respect for one’s society. The general picture that one can draw, following Mumford, is that the element of play was integrated and kept at bay in social rituals. With the transition to empire (traditionally identified with Caesar’s appointment in 44 bc), Rome speeded up its military expansion, or entered what Voegelin (1974) called the ‘ecumenic age’. But here the kinesis, the spiralling logic identified by Polybius and taken up by Voegelin as a key term to identify the moving forces of the entire ecumenic age starts to take an inward aspect as well, reflected in the term invoked by Voegelin, ‘concupiscental expansion’ (1974: 313). This development was very much related to uncontrolled play. The unsettling force in the psychological sense was one of a craving for entertainment, the titillation of the senses, in combinations of liquor, violence and sex. Outward expansionism and internal decay went hand-in-hand in a negative dialectics, pin-pointed with precision by Lewis Mumford in The City in History, as the ‘exploitation of distant territories removes the pressure to achieve balance and harmony nearer at hand’ (Mumford 1961: 242). Mumford sees with clarity how the history of Rome, ‘presents a series of classic danger signals to warn one when life is moving in the wrong direction’ (ibid.). The round-shaped arena here came to play a role which is quite similar to today’s television: The arena, particularly its gladiatorial extravaganzas, combined the contest of sports with the thrill of vicarious violence, even more efficiently than modern TV. But both the arena and the TV perverted the original urban contribution of empathy and mutual understanding into a deadening inability to live except through the lives of others. And where life itself had been most brutalized, the most exciting vicarious thrill was imagining oneself as the mutilator or murderer of others. (Reilly 1980: 85) Liminality and the Modern 154 What we witness here is something very akin to Girard’s sacrificial mechanism imploding within the centre of the world’s then most powerful empire. If one accepts Plato’s view that Athens was morally and politically undermined by sophistry, one might suggest that in Rome this loss of balance was equally connected to the ‘playful’, but also differently so. The play element evidently became incorporated into the spatial design of imperial Rome, where a growing number of public spaces turned into play stages. The most important and most visited public spaces were baths (where erotic games also thrived), arenas and theatres – all scenes of play. Mumford famously wrote that Rome, as an imperial city, became a ‘show-place for power’, and that is true of course, but it went hand- in-hand with the city turning into a show place for play and entertainment. Games became an obsession. This eventually turned into an obsession with blood and death. The Romans were much less interested in Greek-style combat sports where nobody was hurt (it was tried out on the Navona square, with no success at all). Increasingly, blood had to be shed. In the first gladiatorial games in 264 prisoners were publicly executed; but soon this was not enough. Here the parallels to the Aztec empire are striking: in the beginning political prisoners were sacrificed; but at a certain point the relationship is inverted: wars and raids were ventured because prisoners for public execution were needed. The mass killings went on and on, and the style and manner of execution became more and more sophisticated, in order to please the craving audience. Today this public and continuous display of killing and violence has of course been taken over by television and video; we don’t even react anymore when we see people killed in film; it is even funny, but also a bit boring, unless of course a move to some new kind of extreme of violence or evilness, or ‘artistic’ and creative ways of killing with smart weapons, or inflicting new degrees of physical or psychological suffering on the innocent victim. In Rome the development even started to affect the city’s entire economy, because the days on which ludi were held were public holidays, where no business could be conducted. That practically meant that an increasing number of days per year were, by law, dedicated to play and entertainment. The term ‘entertainment industry’ (revealing and frightening as the term is: the machine-like, mimetic production of mass humour) may be modern, but the fact to which it refers less so. The numbers speak for themselves: By the late Republic, ludi were held on 57 days of the year (Bunson 1995: 246); during the reign of Claudius ( ad31–54) there were 93 days of games a year at public expense. By ad 354 the number had risen to 175 such days. Half the year, half their life, Roman citizens spent the day playing, or watching play. By then there were enough arenas and theatres to hold half the population of Rome simultaneously. The social texture of the city and the empire had fatally weakened. Mumford writes that long before its fall, Rome had turned into a necropolis: a city of the living dead. The proliferation of play and games led to what was recognized by an increasing number of people as moral decay, intimately tied to the decline of the entire Roman empire, destroying itself from the inside. There was resistance to this Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 155 development, then as well. At several points in time there was such a craving for games that even emperors who wanted to downsize the phenomenon dared not do so for fear of popular rebellion (this was the case for Hadrian). Games had turned into an obsession. Rome slowly but surely turned into permanent play, and the remarkable thing is that no-one was in charge of this: the development continued as an eroding, de-civilizing force unfolding at the heart of the empire. Also then of course, the cynics and the populists stood ready to defend the derailing: if this is what people want, let them have it. And, it was added: this is good business. Some resistance came from the early church fathers. The plays were seen as standing in contrast to the teaching of Christ, and of course the games were still connected to the veneration of the traditional gods. Christians were advised not to participate in the festivities. But the rejection of these gaming activities also went deeper: it had to do with the negative moral sentiments that they served to stir. The Christian condemnation of play and gambling has to many ‘moderns’ seemed outdated and ridiculous; but one has to understand very carefully what it is that is being rejected, and on what grounds. The early Christians were not against people having a ‘good time’; they literally saw the ‘devil at play’ in the mass-intoxication and fixation with violence, but for obvious reasons, such a critique would easily end in some form of puritan position. This schismogenetic development would remain a constant theme in European history ever since, although it is clear that today the puritan positions have retreated to a minority position without much in the way of political influence. The more general point can be stated in simple terms. If play, pace Huizinga, relates to the creation of culture and is, as Elias argued, intimately linked with the civilizing process, the inverse argument can also be made: a derailed attitude to play is just as intimately part of any de-civilizing process. As Mumford noted with characteristic precision: ‘These are symptoms of the end: magnifications of demoralized power, minifications of life. When these signs multiply, Necropolis is near, though not a stone has yet crumbled. For the barbarian has already captured the city from within. Come hangman! Come vulture!’ (Mumford 1961: 242). It would take a thousand years to recover. Game and Gambling in Early Modernity: The Role of Venice Let us travel with seven-league boots to the fifteenth century, staying however within the Italian peninsula, more precisely Venice and surroundings, the birth- place of institutionalized gambling. The analytical perspectives proposed here in many ways run parallel to the recent argument made by Arpad Szakolczai in Comedy and the Public Sphere (2013), where he suggests a radically different genealogy of the public sphere, moving completely outside established traditions in sociology and political theory. The general point is that we have overlooked a series of cultural practices that became foundational for the emergence of the modern public sphere (or rather, ‘stage’), centuries before the Parisian coffee Liminality and the Modern 156 salons. Szakolczai focuses for very good reasons on the role played by comedy in the late Renaissance or early modernity, as mimes, clowns and comedians came to conquer public squares and spaces. From here, he shows, new forms of ‘denuding’ critique and sarcasm became possible, but often with devastating effects on ordinary and meaningful sociability. The ‘public sphere’, as we are starting to realize these days, is not always such a terribly nice thing, and it can, like markets or states, become perverted by all sorts of power and identity politics. This recognition was in fact also made by Kierkegaard in his astonishingly clear- cut understanding, in the middle of the nineteenth century, that the modern public sphere as produced by the media, was establishing itself as a stage for reciprocal humiliation and de-humanization. The role that gaming and gambling had on this cultural construction of the public stage should not be overlooked. Gaming practices revolutionized cultural and social life in the early modern period, and with particularly rapid effects across the European continent. These cultural developments had enormous impacts on the economy; and they ran parallel to developments in political history that here we can here merely hint at. Venice: Merchant and Ludic Capital of Early Modernity Venice is itself a liminal place in more than one way. It was built on the edge of the inhabitable world, and the building started unplanned. The Roman empire had been devastated by barbarians, and there was no overarching control of the adjacent territories. It was built in the shelter of the lagoon, as a quintessentially interstitial urbanity. It was also due to its in-between position between Europe and the East that it could establish itself as Europe’s leading trade empire. Venice is today a famous tourist attraction. However, the endless beauty of the city should not conceal its historical importance. As discussed in detail by Szakolczai, it was the place where the mimes of Constantinople arrived. It was also in Venice that in 1441 the first recorded large-scale masked festivity took place, establishing the carnival tradition with which the city is still identified today. It was furthermore in Venice that the first staged comedy was performed, in 1431 (Szakolczai 2013: 165). As Szakolczai puts it, ‘Venice was destined to pioneer the rebirth of theatre’ (ibid.: 166), characterized as it was by a manifest sense of ‘theatricality’, omnipresent in the architecture, urban texture and cultural life of the city. Venice’s role as nurturer of the modern public sphere was further underlined by the fact that this was one of the first places in Europe where the printing press started to function. Venice quickly became a centre for the early modern regime of information and communication, following the invention of printing with movable type (Burke 2000). It was precisely because of its liminal position that it achieved this centrality. Venice occupied the position similar to that which California has come to gain in more recent times as a global centre for the joint development of information technology (Silicon Valley), the entertainment industry, and Game and Gambling and the Implosion of Liminality: Playing Modernity 157 a spreading culture of personal ‘freedoms’. It should also not be forgotten that modern banking practices emerged in Venice, and it was from here that the first in a long series of financial crackdowns started. From the period of the First Crusade in 1099 through to the collapse and depopulation of the Black Death in 1347–1351, Europe was in many ways dominated by the Venetian empire. Venice possessed no industry, save for its military-naval factory, the Arsenal. The city’s power lay in its control over trade, particularly trade between Europe and the East, and in banking and currency manipulation. The Venetian-controlled Lombard League was born in the late twelfth century, as the Lombard League cities came to serve as centres for the usurious Lombard banking system. This Venetian-controlled alliance was in command of a financial system based on extreme usury and economic looting. In short, Venice occupies a central space within our general attempts to theorize the transition to modernity. And this is no less the case with respect to the history of gaming and gambling. Venice and the Culture of Gaming: Playing Cards To understand how pivotal a role Venice played with respect to gaming and gambling, one can start by briefly referring to one specific ludic practice, the study of which is normally confined to a very secluded group of historians and esoteric scholars: playing cards. This again of course belongs to a much larger discussion, and we cannot here go into any detail concerning the history of playing cards. While we have full and detailed knowledge of the game of chess, the same cannot be said of cards: an interesting lacuna, in and by itself, considering the pivotal role that cards would come to play in Western culture, culminating in today’s gambling craze, where almost all the games can be traced back, directly or indirectly, to the spread of cards. It is the practice of card playing that has migrated to online gaming and gambling. In fact, an argument can safely be made that from the perspective of cultural history, the introduction of cards is one of the single most important events within the Western world. It is not possible here to enter fully into the endless debates concerning the origin of playing cards. However, we know with certainty that towards the end of the fourteenth century, the practice of card playing slowly started to spread in Europe. It happened suddenly, and then spread rapidly. In his unrivalled comparative study of the tarot game, Dummett identifies 1377 as the pivotal year, where we suddenly find references to card games in Florence, Siena, Basel and Paris. The suddenness of the phenomenon leaves little doubt that cards were introduced from elsewhere in a fully developed form, and then immediately spread over a wide area of Europe (Dummett 1980: 11). The historical documents from the period explicitly talk about packs of cards as something ‘newly introduced’. Out of an apparent void, a string of references emerge pointing to the sudden arrival of playing cards in Belgium, lower Germany, Switzerland, Catalonia and across Italy. Very soon afterwards we hear of them being banned by the authorities all across Europe. Liminality and the Modern 158 Dice and certain board games were already long-established, so playing cards were a new addition to the repertoire of gambling pastimes. But more than any other game, they seemingly brought with them anti-social behaviour and cheating which immediately started to flourish in gaming houses. Violence would often break out, as huge sums were won or lost in no time. This led to bans and prohibitions as preachers demonized the game and the authorities devised ways to regulate the new craze. Most of the historical documentation we have on card playing, in fact, does not refer to its practice (we know it was widespread), but to the banning of it. Playing cards were evidently not invented in Venice, but that this was one of the first places it started to spread and become institutionalized is beyond doubt. Cards almost certainly entered Europe via Venice (Dummett 1980: 45), and there is strong evidence that they arrived to Venice via contact with the Mamluks (Dummett and Abu-Deeb 1973). This is interesting for various reasons. The Mamluks were a quite unique social formation. They were a slave-caste, but over time, and in various places, indeed, at the centres of long-established world civilizations (India, Iraq, Egypt), they developed a strong political power base as a military caste, developing proper dynasties (Waterson 2006). They existed for a thousand years, up until the nineteenth century. From the perspective of global history, the Mamluks must be recognized as a major and in many ways decisive military-political force. However, if playing cards were imports from Islamic lands, it was in Italy (and then in all of Europe) that their symbolic images were replaced by representations of courtly human beings: kings and their attendants – knights (on horseback) and foot-servants. Card playing quickly became established as an extremely prominent and ritualized activity within Italian court society, as in the courts of Ferrara, Milan and Bologna. All of this is of huge sociological relevance. The fuller discussion of this point belongs to a future study, but let us just point to the main aspects. The introduction of cards into Europe involved four fundamental changes from their original usage in the East. First, cards were introduced into Europe at a very significant point in time: with the development of paper manufacturing and the invention of printing, cards were soon to be produced for a mass market and all classes were able to play cards. In 1436 a printing press made specially for cards was imported into the Este court at Ferrara. Via the printing press, cards quickly became objects of mass consumption, whereas in Egypt and China the practice had been restricted to specific, wealthy, social classes. Second, European playing cards introduced a whole series of ‘figures’, displaying an entire symbolic universe of character traits, a marked break from Islamic cards which were beautifully ornamented, but obviously bore no images. Cards are very different from pre-existing games like dice in this respect: they play with images. Many of these images were evidently subversive, grotesque, blasphemous or otherwise provocative. Here one can mention the card known as the ‘Popesse’, one of the main figures in all early Italian tarot games. However, the one figure that is worth pointing out here, was the introduction of the ‘matto’, the ‘crazy one’, ‘la
